title: What getting older actually means
subtitle: Separating the real from the cultural story about ageing
excerpt: Most of what we believe about getting older is cultural narrative, not biology. This reading separates what ageing actually involves from the decline narrative that shapes how people relate to their own age.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from research on ageing, geroscience, and cultural narratives around age. No single source. Framing original to homemade.education.

practice.whenToUse: When a birthday or milestone is bringing up dread or a sense of loss, or when the cultural narrative around ageing is creating anxiety about your own

content.text: The cultural story about ageing runs like this: things were good when you were young, and they get progressively worse from here. Youth is the peak. Everything after is loss. This story is not neutral. It shapes how people experience every decade after their twenties, and it is largely inaccurate.

Longitudinal studies on wellbeing consistently show a U-curve: life satisfaction tends to be high in youth, drops in middle age, and rises again in later life. People in their sixties and seventies, across multiple cultures and income levels, report higher levels of contentment than people in their thirties and forties. This finding is robust and counterintuitive.

What accounts for this? Researchers point to improved emotional regulation, a clearer sense of what matters, less energy spent on social comparison, and a reduced drive for status that tends to ease with age. These are gains, not losses.

Some things do change: physical recovery time, certain kinds of memory speed, and in women, hormonal shifts that affect multiple systems. These are real. They are also not the same as the wholesale decline the cultural story promises.

Research on cognitive ageing shows that while processing speed declines, crystallised intelligence, the accumulated knowledge and judgement that comes from experience, continues to grow well into old age. Wisdom is not a soft concept. It is a measurable shift in how information is processed and decisions are made.

Research on ageing shows that people who hold negative beliefs about ageing die, on average, earlier than people who hold positive ones. The belief about what ageing means affects physical outcomes. The narrative is not just uncomfortable. It has a measurable cost.
Examining your own inherited story about what age means, and separating what you absorbed from what is actually happening, is not naive optimism. It is working with the evidence.
Drawn from geroscience and research on subjective wellbeing across the life course. No single source. By homemade.education.
